text: The proliferation of artificial intelligence across all sectors of society presents significant opportunities alongside profound risks to fundamental rights, health, and safety. In response, the European Union has enacted Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, commonly known as the AI Act—the world’s first comprehensive legal framework for AI governance. This paper examines the architecture of the Act, which shifts away from non-binding ethics guidelines toward a mandatory, risk-based regulatory approach. Central to the Regulation is a classification system that categorizes AI applications into levels of risk: prohibiting practices deemed an "unacceptable risk" to EU values, imposing strict requirements on "high-risk" systems, and establishing specific transparency obligations for generative AI and chatbots. Furthermore, the paper analyzes the Act’s late-stage incorporation of rules for general-purpose AI models and systemic risks, spurred by the rapid ascent of large-scale generative models. By addressing enforcement mechanisms, international extraterritorial effects, and the goal of harmonizing the European internal market, this study considers how the AI Act aims to foster human-centric innovation while establishing a global benchmark for trustworthy artificial intelligence.
